title: Cashew nut yields
subtitle: Yields are measured in tonnes per hectare.
caption: Across the period from 1980 to 2024, cashew nut yields measured in tonnes per hectare display distinct trajectories for Peru, Mali, China, and Mexico, with values ranging from near zero to just under 7 tonnes per hectare. Peru consistently maintains the highest yields among the four nations, beginning around 5.3 tonnes per hectare in the late 1980s, fluctuating between 4 and 5.5 tonnes through the 1990s, and reaching a peak near 6.8 tonnes per hectare around 2005 before settling between 5 and 6 tonnes in the 2020s. Mali shows a gradual increase starting from roughly 1.3 tonnes per hectare in the early 1990s, rising steadily to a peak of approximately 4.2 tonnes per hectare around 2019, followed by a sharp decline to just above 1 tonne per hectare by 2024. China’s line remains low for most of the timeline, hovering between 0.2 and 0.5 tonnes per hectare until a brief spike to nearly 1.8 tonnes per hectare around 2013, then returning to levels near 1 tonne per hectare in the final years. Mexico exhibits high volatility early on, spiking to over 6 tonnes per hectare in 1982, then falling below 1 tonne per hectare by the mid-1990s and remaining under 1 tonne per hectare through 2024. The data shows Peru sustaining yields well above the other three countries across the entire timeframe, while Mali’s rise and subsequent fall creates a visible arc that crosses above China and Mexico temporarily before converging with them at lower levels in the most recent years.
